notes.anchor: the assurance of Laterensis on the spot
notes.note: M. Iuventius Laterensis was a legate attached to Lepidus's army who acted as honest broker, vouching for Lepidus and urging Plancus to trust him; his subsequent disillusionment (sections 3 and 10.23.4) drives him to attempt suicide.

notes.anchor: I had thrown a bridge across the river Isara in a single day
notes.note: The Isara is the river Isère, a left-bank tributary of the Rhone in Transalpine Gaul; bridging it in a day to bring his army toward Lepidus was a feat Plancus advertises as proof of his energy.

notes.anchor: I have sent a copy of his autograph to Titius
notes.note: P. Titius, tribune of the plebs in 43, had earlier vetoed Cicero's motion honoring Plancus; Plancus forwards Laterensis's incriminating autograph letter to him, presumably to vindicate his own conduct before a hostile witness.

notes.anchor: I shall give to Laevus Cispius to carry through, who was party to the whole affair
notes.note: Laevus Cispius is otherwise unknown, a trusted member of Plancus's staff entrusted to carry the originals of the autograph letters as documentary proof.

notes.anchor: men of the Canidius and Rufrenus stamp
notes.note: Canidius and Rufrenus were officers in Lepidus's army whom Plancus blames for corrupting the troops toward Antony; he names them as types of the disloyal commanders he will identify in full later.

notes.anchor: all the enemy declared public enemies at last and their goods confiscated
notes.note: A reference to the Senate's decrees of late April 43 declaring Antony and his followers hostes (public enemies), which entailed confiscation of their property; Lepidus's soldiers cite these very measures as reason not to fight for the Senate's side.
